summary: 6b resolves the bo'el niddah debate: R. Chiyya's baraita explicitly lists bo'el niddah alongside zav, zava, metzora, and tamei meit as daytime immersers - a conclusive refutation. The page then pivots: if we guard the Kohen Gadol against niddah impurity, why not also guard him against corpse impurity? Rav Tachilifa: this proves tumas meit is fully permitted (hutrah) for the tzibbur. Ravina's counter: even if dchuya, corpse impurity is rare while niddah impurity is common - hence only the latter requires precaution. This opens the central amoraic dispute: Rav Nachman says tumas meit in tzibbur is hutrah (no need to seek pure replacements); Rav Sheshet says it is dchuya (must seek purity where possible). The practical question: if some priests in the same mishmar division are pure and others are impure, must the congregation delay service to summon pure priests from another beit av?

display.title: Bo'el niddah immersion settled; corpse impurity question opens hutrah/dchuya
display.oneLine: R. Chiyya's baraita conclusively resolves bo'el niddah immersion as daytime; a new question then opens whether tumas meit in tzibbur is fully permitted (hutrah) or merely overridden (dchuya).
display.shortSummary: The 6a debate is settled when the Gemara cites R. Chiyya's baraita: zav, zava, metzora, metzora'at, bo'el niddah, and tamei meit all immerse by day. A menstruating woman and a woman after childbirth immerse at night. The list explicitly includes bo'el niddah as a daytime immerser - a conclusive refutation (teyvuta). This also confirms, incidentally, that bo'el niddah is more stringent than tamei meit in one respect: he transmits mishkav umoshav impurity (the mild impurity that renders foods and liquids impure), while tamei meit does not. The discussion then turns: if we separate the Kohen Gadol from potential niddah impurity, why not also guard him against corpse impurity? Any visitor might die in his chamber! Rav Tachilifa answers: the omission proves tumas meit is completely hutrah (permitted) for the tzibbur - it needs no special precaution. Ravina's alternative: even if dchuya, corpse impurity is uncommon, whereas niddah impurity can arise at any moment from his wife.
display.whats: R. Chiyya's baraita is the decisive proof the Gemara has been building toward since 6a. It explicitly categorizes bo'el niddah with the daytime immersers, closing the question. But the baraita also triggers a new observation: bo'el niddah is listed as more stringent than tamei meit because he transmits mishkav/moshav impurity (sitting or lying on a surface makes it a rishon le-tumah, capable of rendering food and liquids impure). The argument now pivots to tumas meit. The Kohen Gadol is separated from his wife to avoid niddah impurity. But visitors present a risk of corpse impurity - arguably more severe. Why no protective ordinance there? Two answers emerge: (1) Rav Tachilifa: the omission proves tumas meit simply does not apply in the tzibbur context - it is hutrah. (2) Ravina: even if only dchuya, the practical frequency of the risk justifies treating niddah differently. This sets up the major amoraic dispute in the second sugya.
display.hint: The Gemara uses an argument from silence: 'if you were worried about X, why not also worry about Y?' What does this prove about the underlying legal category of Y?

learning.learnerQuestion: If tumas meit is more severe than niddah impurity, why does the Gemara guard the Kohen Gadol only against niddah impurity and not also against corpse impurity from a dying visitor?
learning.coreTension: Niddah impurity is 'lighter' than tumas meit but receives more regulatory attention. The asymmetry needs explanation. Either there is a deep legal difference (tumas meit doesn't apply to the tzibbur at all), or there is a pragmatic distinction (tumas meit rarely arises from a dying visitor, while niddah uncertainty is an everyday reality).
learning.coreMove: The argument from omission: because the Sages did not institute a guard against visiting-related tumas meit, we can conclude something about the underlying legal status of tumas meit in the tzibbur. Rav Tachilifa uses this argument to support hutrah; Ravina blocks the argument by offering an alternative pragmatic explanation.
learning.resolution: Two valid responses to the omission argument. Rav Tachilifa: it proves hutrah. Ravina: it proves only that frequency matters in rabbinical ordinances. Neither is conclusive - the underlying dispute will be carried into the next sugya.

takeaway.text: Arguments from legislative omission (why no guard against X?) can support either a legal conclusion (X is not legally prohibited) or a pragmatic one (X is too rare to warrant legislation). The Talmud presents both readings without immediately adjudicating between them.

reasoningPattern.notes: The absence of an ordinance is used to infer a legal status. This is a standard Talmudic move: if the Sages would have enacted a precaution had the danger been legally relevant, the absence of the precaution implies the danger is not legally relevant (hutrah). The counter-argument is that pragmatic factors - frequency, feasibility - also shape rabbinic legislation, so the omission need not reflect a legal conclusion.

learning.ahaMoment: R. Chiyya's baraita resolves months of Talmudic debate with a single enumerated list. The deliberate ordering (zav, zava, metzora, metzora'at, bo'el niddah, tamei meit / niddah, yoledet) tells you exactly who immerses by day and who by night - no ambiguity. But the very clarity of the resolution opens the next problem: if bo'el niddah is daytime like tamei meit, and tamei meit is not guarded against in the KG's chamber, something must differentiate them.
learning.learningBlocker: Students may assume tumas meit is always more serious than niddah impurity. The Gemara reveals that bo'el niddah has one stringency over tamei meit: mishkav/moshav transmission. The hierarchy of impurity is context-dependent.
learning.memoryAnchor: R. Chiyya's list of six daytime immersers - zav, zava, metzora, metzora'at, bo'el niddah, tamei meit. Two nighttime: niddah, yoledet. This list is the whole answer.

argumentFlow.label: 6a's objection resolved: baraita 2 teaches impurity, not immersion
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Baraita 2 was comparing baal keri to sherets and bo'el niddah to tamei meit only for impurity duration, not immersion timing. Both durations are written explicitly in the Torah so the comparison must teach something else - the severity level (bo'el niddah is an av hatumah like tamei meit).

argumentFlow.label: Bo'el niddah more severe than tamei meit: mishkav/moshav
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Bo'el niddah is more severe in one respect: he transmits mishkav/moshav impurity (mild impurity making surfaces rishon le-tumah for foods and liquids). Tamei meit does not transmit mishkav/moshav.

sourceRefs.note: The one stringency of bo'el niddah over tamei meit

argumentFlow.label: R. Chiyya's baraita: conclusive refutation
argumentFlow.speaker: Rabbi Chiyya (baraita)
argumentFlow.text: Zav, zava, metzora, metzora'at, bo'el niddah, tamei meit - all immerse by day. Niddah and yoledet immerse at night. Bo'el niddah is explicitly in the daytime group. This is a teyvuta.

sourceRefs.note: R. Chiyya's baraita settling the bo'el niddah immersion question

argumentFlow.label: New question: why not guard against tumas meit from visitors?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: If we separate the KG from his wife to prevent niddah impurity, we should also separate him from visitors - any of whom might die in his chamber and convey tumas meit. Why no such ordinance?

sourceRefs.note: The omission argument opening the hutrah/dchuya discussion

argumentFlow.label: Rav Tachilifa: omission proves tumas meit is hutrah for tzibbur
argumentFlow.speaker: Rav Tachilifa (in name of Rava)
argumentFlow.text: The Sages' failure to legislate against visitor-borne tumas meit proves it is completely permitted for tzibbur service. No precaution needed because no prohibition remains.

sourceRefs.note: Rav Tachilifa's hutrah inference from legislative omission

argumentFlow.label: Ravina: even if dchuya, rarity explains the omission
argumentFlow.speaker: Ravina
argumentFlow.text: Even granting dchuya, corpse impurity from visiting is rare (people don't often die in the KG's chamber), while niddah uncertainty can arise every day with his wife. The Sages legislated against the common risk, not the rare one.

sourceRefs.note: Ravina's pragmatic counter to the hutrah inference

concepts.en: A conclusive refutation - a proof that definitively overturns a position rather than merely creating a difficulty. When the Gemara says 'teyvuta,' the position is considered defeated.
concepts.sugyaRole: R. Chiyya's baraita is declared a teyvuta because it directly and explicitly enumerates bo'el niddah as a daytime immerser, leaving no room for reinterpretation.

concepts.en: Impurity transmitted to a bed or chair even without direct contact. A person carrying mishkav/moshav impurity makes any surface they lie or sit on a rishon le-tumah (first-degree impurity), capable of rendering food and liquids impure. Bo'el niddah has this; tamei meit does not.
concepts.sugyaRole: The one respect in which bo'el niddah is more stringent than tamei meit - answering the question why bo'el niddah needs its own category even when its immersion timing matches tamei meit.

concepts.en: Fully permitted vs. merely overridden. Hutrah: a Torah prohibition is suspended for public service - no need to minimize or seek alternatives. Dchuya: the prohibition remains but is pushed aside by necessity - must still minimize by seeking purity where possible.
concepts.sugyaRole: The central legal question of 6b-7a: which describes tumas meit in the context of tzibbur service? The practical stakes are major: whether pure priests must be sought when possible.

requiresUnderstanding: Bo'el niddah immersion timing established in 6a (R. Zeira's impossibility proof)
The severity levels of different impurities: av hatumah, rishon, etc.
Why tumas meit from a dying visitor is a plausible practical concern for the KG during his seven-day sequestering

misconceptions.misconception: Tumas meit is always more severe than niddah impurity and bo'el niddah.
misconceptions.correction: Bo'el niddah has one stringency over tamei meit: he transmits mishkav/moshav impurity. The hierarchy of impurity severity is multidimensional.

misconceptions.misconception: The absence of an ordinance against visitor-borne tumas meit proves hutrah conclusively.
misconceptions.correction: Ravina shows the omission can be explained pragmatically (rarity) without drawing a legal conclusion about hutrah vs. dchuya.

visualizableElements.description: R. Chiyya's baraita as a two-column table: left column (daytime immersers): zav, zava, metzora, metzora'at, bo'el niddah, tamei meit; right column (nighttime immersers): niddah, yoledet
Bo'el niddah vs. tamei meit: both are av hatumah with seven-day impurity and daytime immersion, but bo'el niddah alone transmits mishkav/moshav
The Kohen Gadol in the Parhedrin chamber with visitors present - one of whom might die - illustrating the corpse-impurity concern that triggers the hutrah/dchuya question

quizSeeds.question: In R. Chiyya's baraita, which of the following immerses at night rather than during the day?
quizSeeds.answer: A niddah and a woman after childbirth (yoledet).
distractors: A zav and a tamei meit
A bo'el niddah and a metzora
A zava and a bo'el niddah

quizSeeds.question: What is the one respect in which bo'el niddah is more stringent than tamei meit?
quizSeeds.answer: Bo'el niddah transmits mishkav/moshav impurity: any surface he lies or sits on becomes a rishon le-tumah, capable of rendering food and liquids impure. Tamei meit does not transmit mishkav/moshav.

quizSeeds.question: Rav Tachilifa argues that the absence of a guard against visitor-borne tumas meit proves what?
quizSeeds.answer: That tumas meit is hutrah (fully permitted) for tzibbur service - so no precaution is needed.
distractors: That corpse impurity is less severe than niddah impurity
That visitors are forbidden in the Parhedrin chamber
That the Kohen Gadol is exempt from tumas meit

display.title: Rav Nachman vs. Rav Sheshet: hutrah or dchuya for tumas meit in tzibbur?
display.oneLine: Rav Nachman holds tumas meit is hutrah for the tzibbur (no need to seek pure replacements); Rav Sheshet holds it is dchuya (must seek purity where possible). The dispute's practical boundary is whether to summon pure priests from another beit av.
display.shortSummary: The amoraic dispute is formally stated: Rav Nachman says hutrah; Rav Sheshet says dchuya. The Gemara then defines where they agree and disagree. Both agree: if pure and impure priests are in the same beit av (priestly division serving that day), the pure ones serve and the impure ones do not. The dispute is whether to go further - if the entire beit av is impure, do they serve as-is (Rav Nachman: yes, hutrah) or must pure priests be sought from a different beit av even though that delays service (Rav Sheshet: yes, dchuya). A second version of the dispute goes further: even when the same beit av has both pure and impure priests, Rav Nachman (in this version) says the impure ones may also serve, since tumas meit is completely irrelevant for tzibbur service.
display.whats: The hutrah/dchuya distinction has precise practical implications. Hutrah: tumas meit in the context of communal Temple service is simply not prohibited - no more than eating matzah on Pesach is prohibited. Dchuya: it remains prohibited but is overridden by necessity - like a pikuach nefesh situation. The consequence: if dchuya, you must minimize exposure to the prohibited situation by seeking pure alternatives. If hutrah, no minimization is required. The first version of the dispute sets the threshold at 'same beit av': both agree pure priests serve when available within the unit; they disagree only on whether to go outside the unit. The second version is sharper: even within the same unit, does the impure priest have standing to serve alongside the pure one? Rav Nachman in the second version says yes - tumas meit is legally irrelevant for tzibbur service.
display.hint: The gap between the two versions of the dispute is significant. Which version makes hutrah more radical? And what theological or legal commitment follows from holding that an impure priest can serve alongside a pure one for a communal offering?

learning.learnerQuestion: What exactly is the practical difference between hutrah and dchuya? Why does a philosophical distinction about 'permission vs. override' translate into such different practical rules?
learning.coreTension: If a Torah prohibition is 'overridden' rather than 'suspended,' it still exists as a background value - and existing background values generate obligations to minimize. If it is 'suspended,' the prohibition has no moral weight in this context and minimization is irrelevant.
learning.coreMove: Map the hutrah/dchuya distinction onto the legislative question: when communal service proceeds with impure priests, does the system actively prefer purity (dchuya: seek replacements) or treat impurity as legally non-existent (hutrah: no preference required)?
learning.resolution: Two versions of the dispute, each with a different threshold. The first version: agree on same-beit-av purity preference; disagree on cross-beit-av search. The second version: even within the same unit, Rav Nachman holds impure priests may serve alongside pure ones.

takeaway.text: Hutrah and dchuya are not just philosophical positions - they encode different legislative programs. Hutrah generates a flat permissive rule. Dchuya generates a 'do the best you can within the exception' rule, which means actively minimizing the prohibited act wherever possible.

reasoningPattern.notes: The hutrah/dchuya distinction appears across multiple Talmudic contexts (not just tumas meit): anywhere a Torah prohibition must yield to another value, the question arises whether the prohibition is fully suspended or merely overridden. The legislative consequences differ systematically: dchuya always generates a residual obligation to minimize.

learning.ahaMoment: The Gemara's 'scope of dispute' analysis is methodologically precise: before letting two Amoraim disagree, the Gemara first establishes what they agree on. Both agree pure priests should serve when available in the same unit. This is not a dispute about basic preference for purity - it is specifically a dispute about how far the obligation to seek purity extends.
learning.learningBlocker: Students may think hutrah means 'it doesn't matter.' It means 'the legal prohibition does not apply in this context' - which is actually a strong positive statement about the structure of the law, not a statement of indifference.
learning.memoryAnchor: Hutrah: seek no replacements. Dchuya: must seek pure priests from another beit av. The whole debate hangs on one question: when the entire serving unit is impure, do you proceed or search?

argumentFlow.label: Formal statement of the dispute
argumentFlow.speaker: Stama de-Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rav Nachman: tumas meit is hutrah for the tzibbur. Rav Sheshet: tumas meit is dchuya for the tzibbur.

sourceRefs.note: Formal statement of the Rav Nachman / Rav Sheshet dispute

argumentFlow.label: Scope: both agree on same-beit-av case
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Where some priests in the same beit av are pure and some are impure, everyone agrees: the pure ones serve, the impure ones do not. The dispute is only about whether to summon pure priests from a different beit av when the entire serving unit is impure.

argumentFlow.label: Rav Nachman: hutrah, no cross-beit-av search needed
argumentFlow.speaker: Rav Nachman
argumentFlow.text: The prohibition is hutrah - completely inapplicable for tzibbur service. When the entire serving beit av is impure, they serve; there is no obligation to delay or import pure priests from another division.

argumentFlow.label: Rav Sheshet: dchuya, must seek pure priests
argumentFlow.speaker: Rav Sheshet
argumentFlow.text: The prohibition is dchuya - overridden by necessity. Because the prohibition still exists, the congregation must seek pure priests from another beit av when possible, proceeding with the impure priests only when no pure ones can be obtained.

argumentFlow.label: Second version: Rav Nachman holds impure priests may serve even alongside pure ones
argumentFlow.speaker: Alternative tradition
argumentFlow.text: A second version of the dispute: Rav Nachman holds that even when pure and impure priests coexist in the same beit av, the impure ones may also serve - since tumas meit is simply inapplicable (hutrah) for communal service.

sourceRefs.note: Second version of the dispute (ikka de-amrei)

concepts.en: The smallest priestly division, a subdivision of the weekly mishmar (watch). On any given day of the week, a specific beit av was assigned Temple service. The hutrah/dchuya dispute turns on what happens when the beit av on duty is entirely impure.
concepts.sugyaRole: The disputed question is whether to summon a different beit av's priests when the duty beit av is impure. Rav Nachman: no. Rav Sheshet: yes.

concepts.en: The practical programs generated by hutrah vs. dchuya. Hutrah: the prohibition does not exist here, so no minimization is required. Dchuya: the prohibition still exists and is overridden - so wherever possible, you must minimize by seeking the permitted alternative.
concepts.sugyaRole: The key to reading the dispute: hutrah means no cross-beit-av search; dchuya means you must conduct that search.

requiresUnderstanding: The mishmar/beit av structure of Temple service scheduling
Why 'overriding' a prohibition creates a residual minimization obligation while 'permitting' does not
The two versions of the dispute and their different threshold points

misconceptions.misconception: Both versions of the dispute are equivalent - they just phrase the same thing differently.
misconceptions.correction: The two versions have different thresholds. The first version: both agree that within the same beit av, pure priests serve and impure ones don't - only the cross-beit-av question is disputed. The second version is more radical: Rav Nachman holds the impure priests may serve even alongside the pure ones.

misconceptions.misconception: Hutrah means the impurity doesn't matter for any purpose.
misconceptions.correction: Hutrah is specific to the communal Temple service context. It means the legal prohibition of tumas meit does not apply to priests performing communal service. It says nothing about other contexts.

visualizableElements.description: Two-panel diagram: hutrah (beit av impure - they proceed, no search) vs. dchuya (beit av impure - summon pure priests from another beit av before proceeding)
The mishmar/beit av hierarchy: one weekly watch divided into daily beit avot, with arrows showing who substitutes for whom under hutrah vs. dchuya

quizSeeds.question: What does Rav Sheshet require that Rav Nachman does not, in the standard version of their dispute?
quizSeeds.answer: Seeking pure priests from a different beit av when the entire serving beit av is impure.
distractors: Delaying service until sunset so impure priests can complete purification
Replacing the impure Kohen Gadol with a backup
Canceling service entirely when too many priests are impure

quizSeeds.question: In the second version (ikka de-amrei) of the dispute, how does Rav Nachman's position go further than in the first version?
quizSeeds.answer: In the second version, Rav Nachman holds that even when pure and impure priests coexist in the same beit av, the impure ones may also serve alongside the pure ones - since tumas meit is simply inapplicable (hutrah) for communal service.

alternateAngles.angle: Philosophical: what does it mean for a prohibition to be 'permitted' vs. 'overridden'?
alternateAngles.text: Hutrah and dchuya are not merely technical categories - they reflect different understandings of how law works when values conflict. Dchuya treats every deviation from the norm as a moral cost that must be minimized. Hutrah treats the Torah itself as defining different normative zones: in communal Temple service, purity is simply not the relevant standard.

glossary.en: Fully permitted vs. merely overridden. When public necessity conflicts with a Torah prohibition, hutrah means the prohibition is suspended entirely in this context. Dchuya means it remains in force but is pushed aside by necessity - and wherever possible, you must return to the permitted path.

The smallest priestly unit assigned Temple service on a given day, a sub-division of the weekly mishmar (watch). The hutrah/dchuya dispute determines whether to summon pure priests from a different beit av when the on-duty unit is entirely impure.
A conclusive Talmudic refutation. Not just a difficulty (kushya) but a definitive takedown - R. Chiyya's explicit list leaves no room for reinterpretation.

Impurity transmitted to surfaces a person lies or sits on. Bo'el niddah has this capacity (making surfaces rishon le-tumah, capable of rendering food and liquids impure); tamei meit does not - explaining why bo'el niddah is considered more stringent in this one respect.

rashiTranslations.en: That makes mishkav and moshav impure: even ten mattresses piled one on top of another. But a tamei meit does not transmit mishkav/moshav - only his direct contact.
To render food and liquids impure: but not to render a person or vessels impure.

Like all others who transmit mishkav/moshav - such as a zav, a zava, a niddah, and a metzora - whose impurity is av hatumah and renders persons and vessels impure. But a bo'el niddah is written (Lev. 15):
All the bedding on which he lies will be impure.
The verse severed him from the severe mishkav/moshav impurity.
Of the niddah herself who renders a person impure, as it is written:
'And whoever touches her bedding etc.'
And brought him to the light impurity of rendering food and liquids impure.

If it were for severe impurity, the verse could have been silent - I would have known that his bedding is impure like the niddah's bedding, since it is written 'and her niddah-impurity shall be upon him.'
Impure like the niddah's bedding, for it is written 'vetehei niddata alav' (her niddah-state shall be upon him).

And before you separate him from the impurity of his house - and all other people enter and leave his presence - separate him from corpse impurity too, so that people not come and go near him, lest a dead person occur upon him.

Permitted for the tzibbur: as it is written 'an individual is postponed to the second Pesach but the tzibbur is not postponed' and Yom Kippur - all its sacrifices are communal, and even his own sacrifice overrides impurity since its time is fixed and its sacrifice depends on him.

Overridden for the tzibbur: even though the communal sacrifice overrides it, it is only with difficulty that it is overridden - it was not completely permitted - and wherever possible to return to purity, we do.
In that beit av: the one serving on that day - everyone is pure and performs the tamidim of that day even though they are communal sacrifices.
Even though the communal sacrifice is involved.
Permission for tzibbur: and we do not seek out others.
Overridden for tzibbur: and we seek out others.
Even where there are pure and impure in that beit av, Rav Nachman disagreed and said: Even the impure may serve.
Because all corpse impurity in the tzibbur the Merciful One has permitted.

I concede where there are remnants for eating: even though the communal sacrifice is offered in impurity, it is not eaten in impurity as we say in 'Keitzad Tzolin' (Pesachim 77b) - and in a minhah that is konetzet (partially consumed) I concede that we return to perform it in purity so that the remnants may be eaten.
And if there is only one: who was not prepared for this.

The bulls, rams, and sheep of the Festival: which are communal sacrifices with a fixed time - and their libation minhah overrides impurity - and it teaches: 'they say bring another' - hence dchuya for tzibbur.
Bull for idolatry: a communal burnt offering, which requires libations as derived in Shelach lecha.
Aaron's ram: of Yom Kippur.

The sheep that accompanies the omer: as written (Lev. 23) 'and you shall do on the day you wave the omer' - and that 'minhah of the sheep' mentioned in the baraita is not the libation minhah but the omer minhah itself that accompanies it, and it is konetzet and its remnants are eaten.

Intentionally not accepted: even though the tzitz atones, the Sages penalized him so that the flesh would not be eaten - but the owners have still atoned, so that non-sacred animals should not be brought to the Temple courtyard, since by Torah law it is valid - but we hear from this that it is not a heiter (permission).
On what does the tzitz atone: it is written 'and Aaron shall bear the sin of the sacred things' - atoning for the owners.
Acceptance: this falls under the category of 'intentional' - but regarding compulsion, 'acceptance/ratzon' is the appropriate term.
Both for the tzibbur: and since the tzitz is needed to effect acceptance, we learn that it is not hutrah.
